subject: ap-calculus-ab
theme: Derivatives reveal the complete story of a function's behavior — where it increases, decreases, reaches extrema, and changes curvature — and existence theorems like the MVT and EVT let us draw rigorous conclusions from limited information.

core_concepts.title: Mean Value Theorem
core_concepts.detail: Mean Value Theorem (MVT): if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$ and differentiable on $(a,b)$, then $\exists\, c \in (a,b)$ such that $f'(c) = \frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}$.

core_concepts.title: Extreme Value Theorem
core_concepts.detail: Extreme Value Theorem (EVT): if $f$ is continuous on a closed interval $[a,b]$, then $f$ attains both an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum somewhere on $[a,b]$.

core_concepts.title: Critical Points and Local Extrema
core_concepts.detail: Critical points occur where $f'(c) = 0$ or $f'(c)$ is undefined; all local extrema occur at critical points, but not all critical points are extrema.

core_concepts.title: Increasing and Decreasing Intervals
core_concepts.detail: Increasing/Decreasing Test: $f' > 0$ means $f$ is increasing; $f' < 0$ means $f$ is decreasing. Analyze the sign of $f'$ on intervals between critical points.

core_concepts.title: First Derivative Test
core_concepts.detail: First Derivative Test: at a critical point $c$, if $f'$ changes from $+$ to $-$ then local max; if $f'$ changes from $-$ to $+$ then local min; if no sign change then neither.

core_concepts.title: Concavity and Inflection Points
core_concepts.detail: Concavity: $f'' > 0$ means concave up (bowl shape); $f'' < 0$ means concave down (arch shape). An inflection point is where $f''$ changes sign.

core_concepts.title: Second Derivative Test
core_concepts.detail: Second Derivative Test: at $f'(c) = 0$, if $f''(c) > 0$ then local min; if $f''(c) < 0$ then local max; if $f''(c) = 0$ then inconclusive.

core_concepts.title: Candidates Test for Absolute Extrema
core_concepts.detail: Candidates Test (closed interval method): to find absolute extrema on $[a,b]$, evaluate $f$ at every critical point in $(a,b)$ AND at the endpoints $a$ and $b$; the largest value is the absolute max, smallest is the absolute min.

formulas.name: Mean Value Theorem
formulas.description: Guarantees that somewhere on $(a,b)$ the instantaneous rate of change (slope of tangent) equals the average rate of change (slope of secant). Think of it as: you must have been going exactly the average speed at some moment on a road trip.
formulas.example: If $f(x) = x^2$ on $[1,3]$: average rate $= \frac{9-1}{3-1} = 4$. MVT says $f'(c) = 4$, so $2c = 4$, giving $c = 2 \in (1,3)$.

formulas.name: Extreme Value Theorem
formulas.katex_string: f \text{ continuous on } [a,b] \implies f \text{ attains absolute max and min on } [a,b]
formulas.description: Continuous functions on closed intervals are guaranteed to have a largest and smallest value. This theorem justifies the Candidates Test — the extrema exist, so we just need to find them. Continuity on a closed interval is essential.
formulas.example: $f(x) = x^2$ on $[-2, 3]$: absolute min at $x=0$ ($f=0$), absolute max at $x=3$ ($f=9$).

formulas.name: Candidates Test
formulas.katex_string: \text{Absolute extrema on } [a,b] \text{ occur at critical points or endpoints: } a, b, \text{ and each } c \text{ where } f'(c) = 0 \text{ or undefined}
formulas.description: To find absolute max/min on a closed interval: find all critical points, evaluate $f$ at each critical point AND both endpoints, compare the values. The largest is the absolute maximum; the smallest is the absolute minimum.

formulas.name: First Derivative Test
formulas.katex_string: f' \text{ changes } + \to - \text{ at } c \Rightarrow \text{local max} \qquad f' \text{ changes } - \to + \text{ at } c \Rightarrow \text{local min}
formulas.description: After finding critical points, make a sign chart for $f'$. If the sign of $f'$ switches from positive to negative, $f$ has a local max at $c$. If negative to positive, local min. If no switch, neither.
formulas.example: $f'(x) = (x-2)(x+1)$: at $x = -1$, f' goes $+ \to -$ so local max; at $x = 2$, f' goes $- \to +$ so local min.

formulas.name: Second Derivative Test
formulas.katex_string: f'(c) = 0 \text{ and } f''(c) > 0 \Rightarrow \text{local min} \qquad f'(c) = 0 \text{ and } f''(c) < 0 \Rightarrow \text{local max}
formulas.description: At a critical point, check the concavity. If concave up ($f''>0$), the function is bending upward so the critical point is a local minimum. If concave down ($f''<0$), it's a local maximum. If $f''=0$, this test is inconclusive.

formulas.name: Concavity and Inflection Point Conditions
formulas.katex_string: f''(x) > 0 \Rightarrow \text{concave up} \qquad f''(x) < 0 \Rightarrow \text{concave down} \qquad f'' \text{ changes sign at } c \Rightarrow \text{inflection point at } c
formulas.description: The second derivative tells you whether the function curves upward (like a bowl) or downward (like an arch). An inflection point is where concavity changes — the curve goes from bending one way to bending the other.
formulas.example: $f(x) = x^3$: $f''(x) = 6x$. At $x=0$: $f''$ changes from negative to positive, so $x=0$ is an inflection point. $f$ is concave down for $x<0$ and concave up for $x>0$.

exam_tip: On AP free-response, always write justifications in terms of the correct derivative — say '$f'$ is increasing on $(a,b)$ because $f'' > 0$ there' rather than using vague language; the College Board awards justification points only for complete derivative-based reasoning.
